title: AI & Prompt Engineering Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of AI, LLMs, prompt engineering techniques, and AI agents.

questions.question: What is the attention mechanism in transformers primarily designed to solve?
options: A. Capture long-range dependencies between tokens in a sequence
B. Speed up training on GPUs
C. Reduce model parameter count
D. Enable training on multiple GPUs simultaneously

questions.question: What is the difference between encoder-only and decoder-only transformer architectures?
options: A. Encoders are faster; decoders are more accurate
B. Encoder-only (BERT) uses bidirectional attention for understanding; decoder-only (GPT) uses causal attention for generation
C. Encoders generate text; decoders classify text
D. They are the same architecture with different names

questions.question: What is RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) used for?
options: A. Making LLMs run faster
B. Training smaller models on large datasets
C. Reducing hallucinations by grounding LLM responses in retrieved relevant documents
D. Random Answer Generation for testing LLM capabilities

questions.question: What is 'temperature' in LLM generation?
options: A. The operating temperature of the GPU during inference
B. The warmup time before a model is ready
C. The number of tokens generated per second
D. A parameter that controls output randomness — 0 is deterministic, higher values are more creative/random

questions.question: Which prompting technique explicitly asks the model to reason step-by-step?
options: A. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting
B. Zero-shot prompting
C. Few-shot prompting
D. Role prompting

questions.question: What is the purpose of RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)?
options: A. Train models to play games by rewarding correct moves
B. Align LLM behavior with human preferences by training a reward model on human rankings
C. Reduce computational cost of training
D. Enable models to learn from internet data automatically

questions.question: What is a vector database used for in AI applications?
options: A. Managing GPU memory allocation during training
B. Storing model weights in a compressed format
C. Storing and querying high-dimensional embedding vectors for semantic similarity search
D. A database specifically for AI-generated code

questions.question: What does 'fine-tuning' an LLM mean?
options: A. Optimizing the model for faster inference without training
B. Fine-tuning hyperparameters without changing model weights
C. Making small adjustments to prompts for better outputs
D. Continuing training a pre-trained model on domain-specific data to adapt its behavior

questions.question: What is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) in the context of LLM fine-tuning?
options: A. A parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that injects trainable low-rank matrices alongside frozen model weights
B. A method to run LLMs on low-RAM devices
C. A library for training large-scale distributed models
D. A compression technique to reduce model size

questions.question: What is the difference between RAG and fine-tuning for adding domain knowledge?
options: A. RAG is faster to train; fine-tuning produces better results
B. RAG retrieves external knowledge at inference time (for dynamic/changing data); fine-tuning bakes knowledge into weights (for style/format)
C. RAG requires more computational resources than fine-tuning
D. Fine-tuning is better for factual recall; RAG is better for creative tasks

questions.question: What is the Chinchilla scaling law's key insight?
options: A. Larger models always perform better regardless of data
B. Models should be trained on data 10x the parameter count in bytes
C. For a given compute budget, training a smaller model on more tokens is often more efficient than training a very large model on fewer tokens
D. Scaling laws only apply to models over 100B parameters

questions.question: What does 'hallucination' mean in the context of LLMs?
options: A. When an LLM generates very creative fictional content on request
B. When an LLM refuses to answer a question
C. When an LLM generates duplicate responses
D. When an LLM confidently generates factually incorrect or fabricated information

questions.question: What is a system prompt in the Claude/OpenAI API?
options: A. Instructions provided to the model before the conversation that define its behavior, persona, and constraints
B. The first user message in a conversation
C. An automated message generated by the operating system
D. A prompt template stored in the database

questions.question: In BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) tokenization, what is a 'token'?
options: A. Always exactly one word
B. A subword unit — could be a character, part of a word, whole word, or multiple words depending on frequency
C. Always exactly one character
D. One sentence of text

questions.question: What is the ReAct prompting pattern?
options: A. Re-reading and reacting to prompts for better understanding
B. React.js framework integration for AI-powered UI
C. Interleaving Reasoning and Acting — model thinks, takes actions (tool calls), observes results, then reasons again
D. A retry mechanism for failed AI responses

questions.question: What is CrewAI primarily used for?
options: A. Single-agent chatbot development
B. Fine-tuning models for customer service
C. Crew management for AI development teams
D. Multi-agent AI orchestration where specialized agents collaborate on complex tasks

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of Ollama for AI development?
options: A. Running LLMs locally on your own hardware with OpenAI-compatible API
B. Access to the latest frontier models like GPT-4
C. Cloud-based model training at low cost
D. Automatic prompt optimization

questions.question: What does 'context window' mean for an LLM?
options: A. The window in which the AI operates on screen
B. The maximum number of tokens (input + output) the model can process in a single inference call
C. The browser window used to access AI APIs
D. The temporal range of training data the model was trained on

questions.question: What is Constitutional AI (used by Anthropic/Claude)?
options: A. AI that can only operate within constitutional law boundaries
B. A government regulation framework for AI deployment
C. A training approach where AI critiques and revises its own outputs based on a set of principles, reducing need for human feedback
D. AI trained exclusively on government constitutional documents

questions.question: In LangChain LCEL (LangChain Expression Language), what does the pipe operator (|) do?
options: A. Performs a bitwise OR operation on model outputs
B. Separates multiple prompts that run in parallel
C. Formats strings in the pipeline
D. Chains components together so the output of one becomes the input of the next

questions.question: What is 'prompt injection' in AI security?
options: A. An attack where malicious content in the input overrides or bypasses the original system instructions
B. Injecting system prompts via API
C. SQL injection adapted for AI databases
D. Injecting additional tokens to extend the context window

questions.question: What is Self-Consistency prompting?
options: A. Ensuring prompts are free of contradictions
B. Sampling multiple chain-of-thought reasoning paths independently, then taking the majority vote answer
C. A prompt that maintains consistent formatting throughout
D. Training a model to be consistent across conversations

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of embeddings in AI/ML?
options: A. Compressing model weights for faster inference
B. Embedding external APIs into model responses
C. Converting discrete data (text, images) into dense numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning
D. Creating visual representations of neural network layers

questions.question: What does 'QLoRA' add to LoRA fine-tuning?
options: A. Quality metrics for evaluating fine-tuned model outputs
B. A quick learning rate adaptation during training
C. Quarterly learning optimization for production models
D. 4-bit quantization of the base model, dramatically reducing GPU memory requirements while maintaining quality

questions.question: What is the Tree of Thoughts (ToT) prompting technique designed for?
options: A. Exploring multiple reasoning paths simultaneously, evaluating intermediate thoughts, and backtracking — best for complex problems
B. Organizing prompt templates in a tree structure
C. Building decision trees from LLM outputs
D. A prompt compression technique for long inputs
